caveats: a: BLS is NAICS 311100, not 311119 or the narrowed outsourced population.
a: Occupation shares are not direct measures of task exposure.
a: Some smart-mill functions reflect conventional automation already deployed and are excluded where identifiable.
rho: Modern-mill examples may not represent smaller legacy facilities.
rho: Reported savings combine downtime, yield, energy, and labor effects.
rho: Regulatory accountability limits full removal of skilled operators and quality staff.
e: Public company descriptions prove the business model exists but do not establish its population share.
e: Mixed pet/livestock supplement firms may classify outside 311119 depending on product and process.
e: Firm eligibility may change if occasional custom mixing is treated as core outsourced revenue.
s5: The examples are selected transactions rather than a frequency study.
s5: One transaction is an asset purchase and another is a recapitalization with owner reinvestment.
s5: Sales size does not map directly to the frozen EBITDA band.
q: No representative contract dataset was found.
q: Retention differs materially by bulk commodity feed, custom formula, supplement, premix, and packaging intensity.
q: Customer concentration and local excess capacity can rapidly force pass-through.
d5: Feed-and-residual grain use is not identical to processed feed shipments.
d5: Higher feed conversion efficiency can reduce feed tonnage per unit of animal output.
d5: Species mix, drought, disease, trade, and herd cycles create substantial regional volatility.
o: The estimate addresses continued need for an outsourced operator, not merely physical feed demand.
o: Vertical integration can displace eligible firms while total feed use grows.
o: Highly automated mills can reduce staffing without eliminating accountable operating entities.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B production priced per ton, batch, formula, package, or supply program for farmers, livestock operators, dealers, and animal-nutrition brands; services may include formulation, blending, pelleting, packaging, testing, traceability, and delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to custom, toll, contract, and private-label production because NAICS 311119 combines outsourced manufacturing with proprietary feed products and captive or integrated mill activity that do not represent external recurring services.
lens.excluded_activities: Proprietary feed brands without material third-party production, captive or on-farm mills, vertically integrated livestock-company mills, ingredient traders, distributors, dog/cat food manufacturers, pharmaceutical makers, and businesses outside the LMM band.
lens.included_activities: Independent custom, toll, contract, and private-label manufacturers of livestock, poultry, equine, aquaculture, and other non-dog/cat feeds, premixes, concentrates, and supplements that repeatedly produce for external customers at roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader four-digit animal-food industry and occupations, not task-level exposure in eligible 311119 custom mills. I map planning, procurement, customer service, documentation, formulation support, quality analytics, inspection review, and predictive maintenance to exposed work, while discounting physical receiving, grinding, mixing, pelleting, sanitation, warehousing, and driving. The four-digit mix includes more consumer-facing pet-food activity and may therefore overstate office-task exposure for commodity feed mills.
caveats: BLS is NAICS 311100, not 311119 or the narrowed outsourced population.
Occupation shares are not direct measures of task exposure.
Some smart-mill functions reflect conventional automation already deployed and are excluded where identifiable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census at 10-15 U.S. custom feed mills, including hours by formulation, scheduling, purchasing, quality, maintenance, load-out, delivery, administration, and customer service, with current automation status identified.
a.rationale: The 2023 BLS mix shows production at 43.30% of employment and transportation/material moving at 20.15%, versus 10.07% office support, 6.80% management, and 3.44% business/financial work. Current feed-mill applications add predictive maintenance, real-time quality detection, demand planning, traceability, and adaptive process control, but much of the payroll remains physical and safety-critical. A little over one-quarter of wage-weighted tasks is a defensible central exposure estimate.

d5.bridge: USDA projects physical corn feed/residual use and animal-product output, not constant-quality demand for outsourced manufactured feed across every 311119 segment. I use corn feed use rising slowly from a 2026/27 base, expanding livestock inventories, steady soybean-meal demand growth, and projected meat, milk, and egg growth as volume anchors, then allow for feed-efficiency gains and on-farm or captive production. Grain disappearance may overstate manufactured-feed demand where farms mix feed internally.
caveats: Feed-and-residual grain use is not identical to processed feed shipments.
Higher feed conversion efficiency can reduce feed tonnage per unit of animal output.
Species mix, drought, disease, trade, and herd cycles create substantial regional volatility.
d5.change_evidence: USDA and Feed Grains Database tonnage by species reconciled to American Feed Industry Association commercial-feed production volumes, split between captive/on-farm and independent manufactured feed for 2026-2031.
d5.rationale: USDA projects corn feed and residual use at 6.0 billion bushels in 2026/27 and then gains of about 20 million bushels per year, supported by recovering cattle inventories. It also projects steady domestic soybean-meal demand growth, 11.9% growth in commercial beef production over the decade, 9.7% milk-production growth, and 8.3% egg-production growth through 2035. These point to low-single-digit five-year feed-volume growth rather than rapid expansion.

caveats: Public company descriptions prove the business model exists but do not establish its population share.
Mixed pet/livestock supplement firms may classify outside 311119 depending on product and process.
Firm eligibility may change if occasional custom mixing is treated as core outsourced revenue.
e.change_evidence: A verified census of U.S. 311119 firms with revenue split among proprietary product, custom/toll manufacture, private label, captive production, distribution, and hauling, matched to normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The code spans complete feeds, supplements, concentrates, premixes, and grain milling for animal feed, much of which is sold under the manufacturer's own brand or produced in captive livestock systems. The eligible subset consists only of firms deriving a recurring majority of activity from custom/toll mixing, contract production, or private-label programs for external customers. Visible U.S. contract manufacturers in supplements and feed exist, but no representative denominator by business model was found; a quarter of LMM firms is a bounded judgment.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311119 ($2,739,442,132) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311119 ($45,523,836,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $1,427,661 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311119); class <5: 234 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,620,769 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $247,167 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 148 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,945,486 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $754,187 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 175 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,728,834 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,788,647 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 188 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,668,851 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,439,500 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 66 firms, avg revenue/firm $113,170,591 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,258,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $440,392,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $67,159,797 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 363

caveats: The estimate addresses continued need for an outsourced operator, not merely physical feed demand.
Vertical integration can displace eligible firms while total feed use grows.
Highly automated mills can reduce staffing without eliminating accountable operating entities.
o.change_evidence: Five-year make-versus-buy plans from major livestock producers and feed brands, new captive-mill and commercial-mill capacity announcements, and shipment data separating outsourced, on-farm, and vertically integrated feed production.
o.rationale: Feed is a physical input and FDA rules require accountable facilities, CGMP, hazard analysis, preventive controls, monitoring, verification, and traceability. Software can optimize formulation and mill control but cannot manufacture, test, load, or deliver the feed. The principal displacement risk is customers bringing production in-house or using captive integrated mills, not software replacing feed; that keeps the operator-required share high but below one for the narrowed outsourced lens.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was found.
Retention differs materially by bulk commodity feed, custom formula, supplement, premix, and packaging intensity.
Customer concentration and local excess capacity can rapidly force pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Twenty or more customer contracts and bid histories showing term, input-index clauses, toll-versus-full-service pricing, open-book provisions, annual tendering, productivity commitments, termination rights, and gross-margin change after efficiency projects.
q.rationale: Per-ton and per-batch pricing permits initial retention of labor savings, especially where formulations, certification, delivery routes, and reliable capacity create switching costs. Commodity-oriented customers are price sensitive, feed inputs are transparent, and agreements may reprice frequently, causing savings to pass through at renewal. Specialized premixes and regulated or private-label programs should retain more than undifferentiated bulk feed.

rho.bridge: Trade evidence describes technical capabilities and reported operational outcomes in modern feed mills, not the five-year implemented share of exposed labor in U.S. LMM custom mills. I convert demonstrated predictive maintenance, quality, demand-planning, and process-control use cases into implementation potential, then discount for sensor coverage, data quality, legacy controls, validation, capital, workforce adoption, and food-safety oversight. Vendor and practitioner case evidence is likely positively selected, biasing potential upward.
caveats: Modern-mill examples may not represent smaller legacy facilities.
Reported savings combine downtime, yield, energy, and labor effects.
Regulatory accountability limits full removal of skilled operators and quality staff.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level implementation records showing sensor readiness, approved capital, deployment time, validation results, operator overrides, and realized labor-hour reductions for comparable LMM feed mills over at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: AI can already interpret vibration, torque, temperature, moisture, quality, and demand data, but the same source states that value depends on structured data, reliable storage, and adequate sensor coverage and that operators remain essential. FDA preventive-control obligations add monitoring, corrective-action, verification, and recordkeeping constraints. About half of exposed work appears implementable in five years, with a wide range across modern automated mills and older regional plants.

s5.bridge: Named feed-mill acquisitions, a family-company recapitalization, and an animal-nutrition acquisition demonstrate control-transfer channels but do not measure five-year probability for the eligible U.S. LMM denominator. I treat the presence of strategic buyers, private equity bids, and family succession as directional evidence, while discounting asset-only deals, product manufacturers outside the service lens, and unknown time at risk. Publicized successful transactions bias probability upward.
caveats: The examples are selected transactions rather than a frequency study.
One transaction is an asset purchase and another is a recapitalization with owner reinvestment.
Sales size does not map directly to the frozen EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, and state feed-license records matched from 2016-2026 to identify qualifying control deals and a surviving denominator, supplemented by owner-age and succession interviews at independent custom mills.
s5.rationale: Cargill bought two U.S. feed mills in 2024 and continued manufacturing for the seller; Graybill was a second-generation family feed-supplement processor that received multiple private-equity bids in a recapitalization; and Anpario acquired a 30-plus-employee U.S. animal-nutrition producer with $8.2 million of sales. These cases show an operative market across assets, family firms, and specialty nutrition, but the eligible denominator and owner-age distribution remain unknown.

principal_driver: Recurring physical feed demand plus practical AI uses in planning, predictive maintenance, quality control, traceability, and mill administration.
principal_weakness: The qualifying outsourced-firm share is unmeasured, and commodity-like per-ton pricing may pass efficiency gains back to concentrated customers.

caveats: S1 is a four-digit occupational-employment proxy, not a wage-weighted task census of eligible 311119 custom mills; the direction and population mismatch are disclosed, but the a range remains judgmental.

S2 is positively selected trade and practitioner evidence about technical capability. It does not measure five-year implemented labor share in U.S. LMM custom mills, so rho remains a broad proxy despite a plausible bridge.

S4 only partly matches the lens because its visible portfolio includes excluded dog and cat products alongside horses, and it establishes neither NAICS classification nor LMM size; it proves business-model existence, not e.

S5 and S6 do not substantiate q: neither provides customer contracts, repricing terms, bid histories, or realized retention of efficiency gains. The packet honestly labels q an ESTIMATE and identifies the missing contract evidence.

S5, S6, and S7 are selected transactions spanning asset purchases, recapitalization, product businesses, and uncertain size fit. They demonstrate transfer channels but cannot estimate s5 or the eligible denominator.

S8 supports physical input and animal-output direction, not outsourced 311119 production or make-versus-buy behavior. The d5 bridge and o estimate disclose this gap, but their narrow high-end durability ranges should not be read as directly observed.

sources_audited.note: The BLS page reports NAICS 311100 employment shares including production 43.30%, transportation/material moving 20.15%, office support 10.07%, management 6.80%, and business/financial operations 3.44%. It does not itself measure task exposure or the narrowed 311119 custom-mill population, as the packet discloses.

The trade article supports predictive maintenance, real-time quality control, sensor and data prerequisites, traceability, demand-linked production, adaptive controls, uneven adoption, and the continued importance of operators. It is interview and practitioner evidence rather than representative U.S. LMM implementation data.

FDA supports facility registration and animal-food CGMP, hazard-analysis, preventive-control, monitoring, corrective-action, verification, receipt/distribution record, and related recordkeeping obligations.

Bimini supports the existence of U.S. private-label and contract supplement manufacturing, including products for horses, but its page centers dogs, cats, and horses and does not establish NAICS 311119 classification, LMM size, or the eligible population share.

Cargill confirms the completed acquisition of two feed mills, dedicated packing lines, livestock and lifestyle/pet customer segments, modernization plans, and continued manufacture for the seller. It demonstrates a transaction and outsourced production but does not measure eligible-firm prevalence, five-year transfer probability, or retention economics.

SC&H confirms Graybill's second-generation family ownership, animal-feed-supplement processing, recapitalization objective, outreach to private-equity firms, multiple competitive bids, and owner reinvestment. It does not provide a transfer-frequency denominator or evidence about pricing and retention.

Anpario confirms the completed Bio-Vet acquisition, Wisconsin production operation, dairy focus, $8.2 million of 2023 sales, and 30-plus employees. The packet appropriately notes that sales do not establish inclusion in the frozen EBITDA band.

USDA supports 6.0 billion bushels of 2026/27 corn feed/residual use followed by roughly 20 million bushels of annual growth, steady soybean-meal demand growth, rebuilding cattle inventories, and projected beef, milk, egg, and other animal-output growth. These measures do not isolate manufactured feed, independent outsourcing, or the operator-required share.
